blurb: An upright blue-green oddball whose fleshy leaves flare into little three- to five-pointed spearheads on erect stems. Powder-dusted and architectural, it slowly builds into a clump that reads more sculpture than houseplant.
size: Erect stems to ~2 ft; spear-shaped leaves 2–4 in long

care.light: I give mine the brightest spot I've got. It colors up to that lovely pale blue in full sun in warm weather, though in the hottest, driest stretches I ease it into part shade so the leaves don't scorch. Indoors, park it right at a bright window for the strongest indirect light you can manage, or the stems stretch and flop.
care.water: Straight soak-and-dry here. I let the mix dry out completely between drinks and water freely through the warm growing months, since it's a summer grower and appreciates the moisture then. Come winter I back right off to roughly one watering a month, just enough that it doesn't shrivel — soggy cold soil is the fastest way to rot it.
care.soil: It wants sharp drainage above all. I use a free-draining cactus/succulent mix and it's happy anywhere from mildly acidic to slightly alkaline; it even shrugs off poorer, sandy soils. The one thing it won't forgive is a mix that stays wet.
care.temperature: This is a tender plant — it prefers to stay above about 40°F and is happiest around 50–55°F. It can take a light, brief frost only if bone dry, but I don't gamble with it: anything near freezing and it comes inside. Roughly USDA Zone 9b and up outdoors.
care.dormancy: It's a summer grower, so it does most of its work in the warm season and idles through winter. I keep it barely watered and on the cool-but-frost-free side over the cold months, then ramp water and feeding back up as spring warmth returns.
care.feeding: Light feeding only, during active growth. I give a dilute liquid succulent fertilizer (a low-nitrogen, bloom-type formula) about once a month from spring through late summer, then stop entirely for winter.
care.potting: I repot only when roots start poking out the drainage holes, moving it into fresh, sandy, well-draining cactus mix. Doing it as the growing season gets going gives it time to settle before it rests.
care.propagation: Wonderfully easy from stem cuttings — I take a cutting in late spring or summer, let the cut end callus and dry for a week or two, then push it into gritty mix and it roots on its own. Division and seed work too, but cuttings are the no-fuss route.
care.bloom: In late summer into early fall it throws up tall, wiry flower stalks well above the foliage, topped with clusters of tiny pom-pom capitula in pale yellow to whitish. They're more curiosity than showpiece — I mostly grow it for the foliage.
care.troubles: Overwatering is the only real enemy — let it sit in wet, cold soil and it rots. I keep drainage sharp and the pot on the dry side, especially in winter, and it stays trouble-free. Handle with a little care, too: the sap can irritate skin.
care.toxicity: Not pet-safe. Like other Senecio it's toxic to cats, dogs, and horses — the ASPCA lists the genus (as "groundsel") for its pyrrolizidine alkaloids, which damage the liver, and the foliage can irritate skin as well. I keep it up out of reach of curious pets.
